coredata.dc:title: Imprinted ratiometric fluorescence capillary sensor based on UiO-66-NH2 for rapid determination of sialic acid
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Pengfei
Tang, Sisi
Wu, Xiaodan
Chen, Yu
Tang, Kangling
Fu, Jinli
Lei, Huibin
Yang, ZhaoXia
Zhang, Zhaohui
coredata.dc:description: Development of method with micro-volume reagent usage and high selectivity for rapid determination of valuable disease-related biomarkers is of great importance for disease surveillance. Herein, a novel ratiometric fluorescence capillary imprinted sensor was developed for highly selective fluorescence determination of sialic acid (SA) based on luminescent metal-organic framework (UiO-66-NH2). In this strategy, the imprinted pre-polymer with double emissions was sucked into the capillary to grow imprinted layer on the inner wall of the capillary. In presence of SA, the blue fluorescence (440 nm) served as fluorescence response signal was quenched due to the electron transfer mechanism between metal organic framework and SA while the red fluorescence (655 nm) served as the reference signal was remained. Within SA concentration linear range of 1.0 × 10−7–3.0 × 10−4 M, the ratiometric fluorescence capillary imprinted sensor was applied to detect SA in human serum and saliva samples successfully with a detection limit of 2.5 × 10−8 M. A sensitive and selective fluorescence method with micro-volume sample consumption (15 μl) for determination of SA in practical samples was established.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence capillary
Ratiometric fluorescence
Molecularly imprinting polymer
Sialic acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Validation by high performance liquid chromatography
Effect of incubation time and pH
Analytical performance of MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary sensor
Fluorescence response of MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary sensor toward SA
Repeatability, stability and reusability of the ratiometric fluorescence capillary imprinted sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) SEM images of CdTe@SiO2, (B) UiO-66-NH2, (C) MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP, (D) MOF/CdTe@SiO2@NIP, (E) the cross section of MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary.
(A) XRD patterns of MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP, MOF/CdTe@SiO2@NIP and UiO-66-NH2. (B) FT-IR spectra of MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP, MOF/CdTe@SiO2@NIP and UiO-66-NH2.
(A) Effect of response time of MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary toward SA on I440/I655, (B) Effect of pH value on the response of MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary of SA.

(A) Effects of interfering ions and SA on the fluorescence intensity of the MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary. (B) Influence of the coexistent ions on the fluorescence intensity ratio of MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary in the present of SA. (C) Changes of fluorescence intensity ratio of MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary and MOF/CdTe@SiO2@NIP capillary toward SA and analogues.

(A) The fluorescence spectra of MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary toward SA with different concentrations; (B) Linear relationship of MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary toward SA; (C) Fluorescence spectra of MOF/CdTe@SiO2@NIP capillary toward SA with different concentrations; (D) Linear relationship of MOF/CdTe@SiO2@NIP capillary toward SA.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of UiO-66-NH2, SA and UiO-66-NH2+SA; (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of SA and the fluorescence emission spectrum of the UiO-66-NH2; (C) schematic illustrations for the quenching mechanism.
Schematic illustration of the synthetic procedure and determination of MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary.
Spiked recoveries and RSD (%; n = 3) for the determination of SA in real sample by the MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary and HPLC-UV.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a,b The samples were 100 times diluted.
c P: P > 0.05 indicating no significant difference.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Performance comparison of different methods for the determination of SA.

head.ce:title: Imprinted ratiometric fluorescence capillary sensor based on UiO-66-NH2 for rapid determination of sialic acid

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Development of method with micro-volume reagent usage and high selectivity for rapid determination of valuable disease-related biomarkers is of great importance for disease surveillance. Herein, a novel ratiometric fluorescence capillary imprinted sensor was developed for highly selective fluorescence determination of sialic acid (SA) based on luminescent metal-organic framework (UiO-66-NH2). In this strategy, the imprinted pre-polymer with double emissions was sucked into the capillary to grow imprinted layer on the inner wall of the capillary. In presence of SA, the blue fluorescence (440 nm) served as fluorescence response signal was quenched due to the electron transfer mechanism between metal organic framework and SA while the red fluorescence (655 nm) served as the reference signal was remained. Within SA concentration linear range of 1.0 × 10−7–3.0 × 10−4 M, the ratiometric fluorescence capillary imprinted sensor was applied to detect SA in human serum and saliva samples successfully with a detection limit of 2.5 × 10−8 M. A sensitive and selective fluorescence method with micro-volume sample consumption (15 μl) for determination of SA in practical samples was established.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The determination of disease markers in body fluids or tissues plays an important role in the early warning of diseases. Sialic acid (SA), an N-acetylated derivative of neuraminic acid, is found at the terminal position of glycoproteins and glycolipids on the cell membranes, which play crucial roles in various physiological and pathological processes [1]. When normal cells undergo pathological changes, SA will be overexpressed on the cell surface. SA levels in serum or saliva is closely related to many diseases, which has been defined as a promising biomarker for cardiovascular disease, diabetes, degenerative conditions and cancer [2–5]. Thus, the measurement of SA in serum or saliva could be valuable in earlier diagnosis of malignant disease or monitoring the treatment effect of tumor. Currently, some methods have been developed to detect SA, such as colorimetry [6,7], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [8], electrochemistry [9–13], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [14] and fluorescence [15–19]. Although these methods have accurate results, some shortcomings involving expensive equipment, easy interference, time consuming process and large amount of reagent consumption limited their applications. Interestingly, fluorescence determination method has attracted much attention due to its simple operation, rapid response and high sensitivity [20]. Currently, various materials have been used as fluorescence probes for SA fluorescence determination, such as carbon dots [15], gold nanoparticles [16], reduced graphene oxide (rGO) [17] and luminescent metal-organic frameworks (LMOFs) [18,19]. Although these methods have outstanding selectivity, long response time of sensor limits their applications. Moreover, the large consumption of reagent or sample is another issue. It is meaningful to establish a sensitive and selective method with micro-volume sample consumption for rapid determination of SA.

Fluorescence capillary analysis (FCA) which used the glass capillary instead of the traditional cuvette can achieve trace-level assay on the fluorescence detector (<18 μl/time) [21]. This method not only lessens the waste of chemical reagents, but also reduces the sampling volume of biological samples. Recent years, an immobilized FCA method has been successfully used in determination of pyruvic acid and glucose in serum and trace-level nitrite in food samples [22,23].

Fluorescence molecularly imprinted polymers (FMIPs) are a series of polymers combing the outstanding selectivity of molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) and the excellent sensitivity of fluorescence analysis, which exhibit excellent sensitivity, selectivity and stability [24,25]. Chen et al. developed an eco-friendly near infrared fluorescence molecularly imprinted sensor based on zeolite imidazolate framework-8 for rapid determination of trace trypsin [26]. Capillary fluorescence imprinting detection technology combined the advantages of the two technologies to achieve the micro-volume, rapid and high selective determination analyte. Tang et al. proposed a near-infrared fluorescence capillary imprinted sensor for chiral recognition and sensitive determination of L-histidine [27]. However, the reported fluorescence sensor provided only a single emission feature, which was susceptible easily by environmental conditions and interfering substance. Interestingly, ratiometric fluorescence sensor with dual-emission signals was proposed, which can eliminate the interference of single fluorescence sensing by self-calibration of dual-emission to achieve accurate determination [28].

Currently, LMOFs have been developed rapidly in recent years, becoming a promising fluorescence material and exhibiting broad application in the area of fluorescence sensing and imaging [29,30]. When LMOFs were introduced into FMIPs, the response time will be shortened and the fluorescence response characteristics will be enhanced due to its large specific surface area [31]. In this paper, an multi-color and uniformly imprinted ratiometric fluorescence (MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP) capillary sensor for the rapid determination of SA has been developed, in which the LMOFs (UiO-66-NH2) were used as the responsive signal material and the red CdTe quantum dots coated with silicon layer (r-CdTe@SiO2) were served as the reference signal material. Firstly, the pre-polymer solution was prepared by sol-gel method. Then, the pre-polymer solution was sucked into the capillary to grow an imprinted microsphere layer on the inner wall of the capillary for the preparation of MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary sensor. The novel ratiometric fluorescence capillary imprinted sensor possessed a rapidness, excellent selectivity and strong anti-interference ability for the determination of SA in human serum and saliva samples.

N-Acetylneuraminic acid (SA, 98.0%), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.5%), zirconium chloride (ZrCl4) and 2-amino terephthalic acid were obtained from Macklin Biochemical Company (Shanghai, China). Glucose, tryptophan, uric acid, D-(+)-galactose and estrone were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). 3-Aminopropyltriethoxy silane (98.0%, APTES), 3-mercapto-propyltrimethoxysilane (95.0%, MPS), tetraethoxysilane (98.0%, TEOS) and ammonium hydroxide (25.0%, NH3∙H2O) were purchased from Aladdin Regent Company (Shanghai, China). Tellurium powder (99.9%), cadmium chloride hydrate (99.0%, CdCl2·2.5H2O) and sodium borohydride (98.0%, NaBH4) were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). Anhydrous ethanol (99.7%), methanol (99.7%) and hydrochloric acid (36.0%) were purchased from Jinshan Chemical Reagent Company (Chengdu, China). Phosphate buffer saline (PBS) solution was prepared by mixing sodium hydrogen phosphate (0.01 M) and sodium dihydrogen phosphate (0.01 M). Glass capillary (0.9 mm × 80 mm) was purchased from West China Glassware Factory (Sichuan University).

Fluorescence spectra were analyzed on a F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra (UV–vis) were performed on a 1780 UV–vis absorption spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Ultrasonic process was carried out in an ultrasonic bath (40 kHz, Bransonic). The centrifugation operation was performed on a Neofuge 15 high-speed centrifuge (China). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was characterized on a JEM 2100 F (JEOL, Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed on a TD3500 (Dandong Tongda, China). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured using a Nicolet iS10 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermoscientific, USA).

The water-soluble CdTe QDs was prepared according to the previous method with some modifications [27]. Firstly, 3.8 mg of tellurium powder and 10 mg of NaBH4 were dissolved in 5 ml of ultrapure water under nitrogen atmosphere by sonicated for 60 min to obtain NaHTe precursor solution. Meanwhile, 40 μl of MPA was added into ultrapure water containing 53.2 mg CdCl2, in which the solution pH was adjusted to 13.0 with 1 mol L−1 of NaOH solution. Then, both the above solutions were mixed quickly and refluxed at 100 °C for 45 min in water bath under nitrogen atmosphere. After cooled, the prepared red CdTe QDs solution was kept in a refrigerator at 4 °C for future experiments.

The CdTe@SiO2 was prepared according to the previous method with some modifications [27]. Firstly, 25 ml of ethanol and 5 ml of red CdTe QDs solution were magnetically mixed for 20 min. Then, 10 μl of MPS was added to the mixture solution and magnetically stirred for 12 h. After that, 120 μl of APTES and 120 μl of TEOS were injected into the mixture solution. Magnetically stirred for 6 h, the mixture solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min. The resultant precipitate was washed with methanol and water several times. Finally, the precipitate was dispersed into 5 ml of methanol for the subsequent experiments.

UiO-66-NH2 nanoparticles were prepared according to the method with minor modifications [32]. Briefly, 0.3 g of ZrCl4 (1.287 mmol) and 0.2332 g of 2-aminoterephthalic acid (1.287 mmol) were dissolved in a mixture solution containing 60 ml of DMF and 0.19 ml of ultrapure water at room temperature. After stirred at room temperature for 20 min, the mixture solution was transferred to a 100-ml Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 120 °C for 24 h. Cooled to room temperature, the product was collected by centrifugation (6000 rpm) and washed repeatedly with ethanol and DMF solution three times. Finally, the precipitation was dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C to obtain UiO-66-NH2 nanoparticles.

The MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary was obtained according to previous method with some modifications [33,34]. The glass capillary was immersed firstly in 1 M NaOH solution for 4 h, and rinsed with ultrapure water. Then, the glass capillary was immersed in 1 M hydrochloric acid for 4 h. After the capillary was dried with nitrogen gas, APTES dissolved in methanol (1/50, v/v) was injected into the capillary with a syringe. Sealed with rubber, the capillary was kept at 40 °C overnight. Finally, the capillary was rinsed with methanol and water successively to flush out the residual reagents and dried again for further experiments.

1 mg of UiO-66-NH2, 3 ml of CdTe@SiO2 solution, 100 μl of APTES and 5 mg of SA were dissolved in 5 ml of porogenic solvent containing methanol and acetonitrile (9/1, v/v) to form homogenous solution. After the mixture solution was stirred for 30 min, 100 μl of TEOS and 100 μl of NH3·H2O were added and magnetic stirred at room temperature for 6 h to form the pre-polymer. After that, the pre-polymer was siphoned into the pretreated glass capillary, which was incubated at room temperature for 24 h to grow a thin fluorescence imprinted layer (MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP) on the inner wall of capillary. Finally, the cleaning solution containing methanol and ultrapure water (8/2, v/v) was sucked into the capillary several times to remove the template until SA was not detected by UV–vis to obtain the fluorescence capillary imprinted sensor (MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary). The fluorescence capillary non-imprinted sensor based on CdTe QDs and UiO-66-NH2 (MOF/CdTe@SiO2@NIP capillary) was synthesized by the same process without adding SA.

All fluorescence determination was performed on F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer under the same conditions. An excitation wavelength is 360 nm and a photoelectric multiplier is 550 V with a slit width of 10 nm for both excitation and emission. The emission wavelength range is 400–688 nm. Due to the self-imbibition property of the capillary, 15 μl of SA solution was inhaled by the MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary once time. After incubation for 2.5 min, the MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary was placed in the special fixing holder which was inserted into the dark compartment of F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The whole procedure was repeated three times and the average value was used.

Human serum and salivary samples were obtained from healthy volunteers and processed according to previous reports [7,35]. Briefly, 5.5 μl of concentrated H2SO4 was added into 994.4 μl of human serum sample. After kept at 80 °C for 1 h, the sample was neutralized with 0.5 M of KOH solution. The treated serum sample was diluted 100 times with PBS solution (pH = 7.0). Finally, SA solutions with different concentrations were added into serum samples to prepare the spiked samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation by high performance liquid chromatography
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the reliability of the experimental results, high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) was used to validate the determination results [36]. The chromatographic pump (LC-20AR, Shimadzu, Japan), diode array detector (SPD-M20A, Shimadzu, Japan), standard curve method for quantification based on peak area, and C18 reversed-phase column (5 μm, 20 × 250 mm, Shimadzu, Japan) were used for separation. The mobile phase was the mixture solution containing water, methanol and acetonitrile (85/7/8, v/v/v). The wavelength was 220 nm, the injection volume was 20 μl, the column temperature was 35 °C, and the flow rate was 1 ml min−1.

The preparation process of MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary was shown in Scheme 1 . Firstly, UiO-66-NH2 as response signal material, which can emit blue light at 440 nm, was synthesized with a solvothermal method. CdTe@SiO2 as reference signal material was coated by a layer of silica on the surface of CdTe by sol-gel progress with the emission wavelength at 655 nm. Then, UiO-66-NH2 and CdTe@SiO2 were used as two fluorescence resources of ratiometric fluorescence capillary imprinted sensor, which showed excellent fluorescence performance for the determination of SA. After that, a fluorescence imprinted pre-polymer was prepared by mixed with APTES as functional monomer, TEOS as crosslinking agent, NH3∙H2O as catalyst, two fluorescence sources and SA. After that, the pre-polymer solution was sucked into the pretreated capillary to form a uniform thin layer on the inner wall of capillary. After removal of the template, a thin fluorescence imprinted layer was deposited on the inner wall of capillary. The emission peaks of MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary were 440 nm and 655 nm under excited at 360 nm. When SA was rebounded to the imprinted sites, the fluorescence intensity of the blue emission at 440 nm decreased and red emission at 655 nm remained stable.

To obtain a uniform imprinting layer in the inner of capillary, one of the most important parameters is the porogenic solvents. Studies showed the thickness and structure of the resulting imprinted layer are closely depended on the properties of the porogenic solvents [37]. Currently, the commonly used porogenic solvents included toluene, acetonitrile, dodecanol, cyclohexanol and some mixed solvents. In addition, the ratio of porogenic solvents also affects the uniformity of imprinted layer. The results showed the higher of the proportion of porogenic solvent used, the more of the proportion of large pore in the imprinted layer obtained. When the proportion of porogen is less than 5% (v/v), the imprinted layer of MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary is thick and irregular. Increasing the proportion of porogenic solvents can increase the uniformity of the imprinted layer. However, when the proportion of porogenic solvents is over 15%, the imprinted layer begins to fall off easily, which was similar with the previous report [38]. In this paper, the uniformity imprinted layer was obtained when 8%–10% of porogenic solvents was used for the preparation of imprinted capillary. Fig. S1 shows the SEM images of cross section of MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary prepared with three different porogenic solvents. As shown in Figs. S1A and an uneven and cracked imprinted layer appears when only methanol was used as porogenic solvent. As shown in Fig. S1B, although the imprinted layer is relatively uniform, the imprinted layer will fall off in some places. It can be deduced that binary porogen is better than single porogen in forming uniform imprinted layer. Fig. S1C indicated that the imprinted layer with uniform and porous in the capillary favored the accuracy of fluorescence determination.

The SEM image of CdTe@SiO2 shown in Fig. 1 A indicated that the composite material has spherical structure with the average diameter of 90–100 nm. The SEM image of UiO-66-NH2 synthesized by the solvothermal method was shown in Fig. 1B. The UiO-66-NH2 particles are of an octahedral shape with the average sizes of 150–200 nm, which was similar with the reported literature [32]. The SEM images in Fig. 1C and D showed that the prepared MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP and MOF/CdTe@SiO2@NIP was uniform and porous spherical with a particle size of 450–500 nm. As shown in Fig. 1E, the cross section of SEM image of the MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary showed that the imprinted microspheres were deposited evenly on the inner wall of the capillary with the thickness of 50 μm.

The XRD patterns of UiO-66-NH2, MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP and MOF/CdTe@SiO2@NIP were shown in Fig. 2 A. The XRD pattern of UiO-66-NH2 shown in the inset graph agreed with previous report [39], which exhibited the successful synthesis of UiO-66-NH2. The XRD patterns of MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP and MOF/CdTe@SiO2@NIP were different from UiO-66-NH2 indicated their crystallinities were changed obviously after imprinted. Moreover, one broad characteristic diffraction peak (2θ = 23°) of amorphous SiO2 was observed, which was similar to those of literature data [40]. These data suggested the successful preparation of imprinted polymer layer on the capillary.

Fig. 2B presents the FT-IR spectra of UiO-66-NH2, MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP and MOF/CdTe@SiO2@NIP. Zr–O–H group vibration (1386 cm−1) and C–O–Zr group vibration (1400 cm−1 and 1571 cm−1) were belonged to UiO-66-NH2 [41]. The peak at 3357 cm−1 was attributed to bond vibration of -NH- group [18]. The characteristic peaks at 1390 cm−1 and 1649 cm−1 were ascribed to C–O stretching bands and CO stretching vibrations of carboxyl group, respectively. These data were similar to those previous literature [28], indicating the successful synthesis of CdTe QDs. The peak at 1050 cm−1 was belonged to Si–O–Si stretching vibration, which was derived from APTES and TEOS. The characteristic peak at 2939 cm−1 was associated with C–H stretching and bending vibrations induced by APTES functional monomers [42]. These data confirmed the successfully synthesis of the fluorescence imprinted layer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of incubation time and pH
ce:section.ce:para: The incubation time of MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary and MOF/CdTe@SiO2@NIP toward SA was evaluated. As displayed in Fig. 3 A, the values of I440/I655 (I440 and I655 are the fluorescence intensity of the capillary at the wavelength of 440 nm and 655 nm, respectively) decreased rapidly from the beginning of response to 1.5 min, and the fluorescence intensity decreased gently from 1.5 min to 2.5 min. The I440/I655 values tended to be stable after 2.5 min. In addition, the fluorescence quenching effect of MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary was higher than that of MOF/CdTe@SiO2@NIP capillary. Therefore, 2.5 min was chosen as the incubation time for further experiments.

The pH of solution may influence the fluorescence response of MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary toward SA. Fig. 3B shows the I440/I655 values of MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary at different pH values. Under different pH conditions, the I440/I655 values of the MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary were stable. When SA was added, the optimized I440/I655 value of the MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary at pH 7 was obtained. While the pH value was lower or higher than pH 7, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary by SA decreased gradually. When the solution pH was lower than 7, the sensitivity of MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary decreased, which was attributed to that the existence of H+ affected the hydrogen bonding between the MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP and SA [43]. When the pH value was higher than 7.0, the recognition sites on the surface of the imprinted polymers were destroyed. Thus, the interaction between the imprinted binding site and the template reduced, resulting in the decrease of fluorescence quenching effect [43,44]. Therefore, PBS solution (pH = 7.0) was selected for further experiment.

The influence of interfering ions (such as Na+, K+, NH4 +, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Fe2+, NO3 −, Cl−, SO4 2−) on the fluorescence performance of ratio fluorescence capillary imprinted sensor was studied. As shown in Fig. 4 A, when SA and interfering ions with the same concentration were added respectively, the I440/I655 value of MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary reduced by SA more significantly compared by these interfering ions. As depicted in Fig. 4B, the fluorescence intensity of the MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP changed significantly when only SA was present. However, when the different ions were added together with SA, the fluorescence intensity ratio changes of the MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary was similar with alone SA. The above results indicated the developed sensor showed satisfactory specificity. In addition, the selectivity of the fluorescence capillary imprinted sensor was discussed by comparing the fluorescence quenching efficiency of MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary sensor toward SA and five chemical structure analogues involving glucose, tryptophan, uric acid, D-(+)-galactose and estrone. As shown in Fig. 4C, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of MOF/CdTe@SiO2@NIP capillary toward SA and other structural analogues was similar. By contrast, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP toward SA was higher than that toward other structural analogues, which indicated the high selectivity of MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP. The high selectivity was attributed to the imprinted recognition sites matched with SA in term of size, space characters and functional groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary sensor
Fluorescence response of MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary sensor toward SA
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence response performance of MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary sensor toward SA with the different concentrations range of 0–3.0 × 10−5 M was investigated. As shown in Fig. 5 A, when SA solution was sucked into the MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary, the fluorescence intensity at 440 nm gradually decreased, while the fluorescence intensity at 655 nm remained stable, which resulted in the fluorescence intensity ratio (I440/I655) of the MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary sensor decreased with the increment of SA concentration. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity ratio (I440/I655) of the MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary sensor was linear with SA concentrations within range of 1.0 × 10−7 M–3.0 × 10−4 M. As shown in Fig. 5B, the linear regression equations were y = 1.698–0.0267x (R2 = 0.996) and y = 1.638–0.0014x (R2 = 0.997) in the concentration ranges of 1.0 × 10−7 M–1.0 × 10−6 M and 1.0 × 10−6 M–3.0 × 10−4 M, respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) of the MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary toward SA was calculated as 2.5 × 10−8 M (LOD = 3δ/S, S is the slope of the fitting curves and δ is the standard deviation of the blank), which is lower than that of previous reported sensors [6,8,9,12–15,18,19]. The fluorescence intensities of two fluorescence capillary sensors decreased with the addition of SA, but the quenching effect of MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary was stronger than that of MOF/CdTe@SiO2@NIP capillary, which was attributed to the specific imprinted sites existed on the surface of MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary matched with template molecules in function groups, structure and size.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of UiO-66-NH2, SA and UiO-66-NH2+SA were shown in Fig. 6 A. Since no other new absorption peak generated in UiO-66-NH2+SA system, indicating no new complexe was formed between UiO-66-NH2 and SA, the static quenching mechanism was excluded in this quenching mechanism. Fig. 6B shows that the absorption spectra of SA and the emission spectra of UiO-66-NH2 are not overlap, indicating the fluorescence resonance energy transfer mechanism can be excluded for explain the fluorescence response of the UiO-66-NH2 toward SA [28]. In addition, there was no overlap between the UV–vis absorption peak of SA and the fluorescence emission peak of UiO-66-NH2, suggesting that the electron transfer between SA and UiO-66-NH2 was ascribed to the fluorescence quenching of UiO-66-NH2. According to the literature [28,45], the irradiated UiO-66-NH2 by ultraviolet light can induce electron transfer from the ligand to the metal center, namely the transition of electrons at terephthalate HOMO level to Zr-related LUMO level, resulting in UiO-66-NH2 luminescence. As shown in Fig. 6C, the electrons of UiO-66-NH2 in the MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP (in the valence band) accepted the UV energy, and they were excited to the conduction band. With the absence of SA, these electrons would return to the ground state with energy releasing, in which they were emitted fluorescence. When SA molecules are specifically recognized by molecularly imprinted polymers, the excited electrons of the nanoparticle (donor) transfer to the high energy orbits of the SA (acceptor) and become a part of the SA electrons. Therefore, it can be presumed that the amino group on UiO-66-NH2 and SA in the imprinted cavity were bonded by hydrogen bond, which the excited electrons were transferred to SA by hydrogen bond, resulting in the most possible fluorescence quenching of UiO-66-NH2 via electron transfer process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Repeatability, stability and reusability of the ratiometric fluorescence capillary imprinted sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the practicality of the ratiometric fluorescence capillary imprinted sensor, the reproducibility, reusability, and stability of MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary were studied. Firstly, ten MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillaries were prepared under the same procedure. Six capillaries were randomly selected to discuss their fluorescence responses toward 5.0 × 10−6 M SA solution. As displayed in Fig. S2A, the I440/I655 values of the MOF@CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillaries toward SA were similar (RSD = 4.6%, n = 6), which indicated the MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary sensor possessed excellent reproducibility. Then, the fluorescence response of the same MOF@CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary toward 5.0 × 10−6 M SA solution was detected once a week. As displayed in Fig. S2B, the fluorescence response toward SA was consistent (RSD = 2.3%, n = 5) within four weeks. The results showed that the MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary sensor had excellent stability. In addition, the fluorescence response of MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary toward 5.0 × 10−6 M SA was detected, and then removal of SA from the imprinted capillary with methanol/ultrapure water (8/2, v/v). After repeated 9 cycles, as shown in Fig. S2C, the RSD was 4.3%, which demonstrated that the MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary possessed excellent reusability.

In order to verify the application of the ratiometric fluorescence capillary imprinted sensor, the MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary was used for detecting SA in saliva and blood samples based on the standard addition method. Each sample was performed to three parallel determinations, and the results were compared with the HPLC-UV method. Descriptive analysis and difference significance test of statistics method (Student's t-test, P > 0.05) were adopted in final results analysis. As shown in Table 1 , the recoveries of SA in saliva and blood sample ranged from 98.0% to 102.8% with the RSD (n = 3) values less than 5%, which was similar with the results of the HPLC-UV method. Meanwhile, the applicability of the method was further confirmed by the difference between the two results in terms of the student's t-test (P > 0.05). No significant difference between the determination results of HPLC method and the MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary fluorescence sensor indicates the MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary is credible for the determination of SA in complex substrates.

The performance of the MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary was compared with previous reported methods for the determination of SA. As shown in Table 2 , the developed MOF/CdTe@SiO2@MIP capillary sensor displayed lower detection limit and shorter response time. Meanwhile, the ratiometric fluorescence capillary imprinted sensor achieved higher sensitivity and selectivity for the determination of SA with the micro-volume reagent and sample consumption.

A novel dual-emission fluorescence capillary imprinted sensor based on luminescence metal organic framework was developed for highly sensitive and selective determination of SA with the micro-volume sample consumption. The ratiometric fluorescence capillary imprinted sensor exhibited lower LOD of 2.5 × 10−8 M toward SA and shorter response time (2.5 min). Moreover, the ratiometric fluorescence capillary imprinted sensor was applied successfully to rapid determination of SA in human serum and saliva samples.

Pengfei Zhao: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing-Original draft. Xiaodan Wu: Resources. Sisi Tang:Data Curation. Kangling Tang: Formal analysis. Yu Chen: Validation. Jinli Fu: Supervision. Huibin Lei: Methodology. Zhaoxia Yang: Supervision. Zhaohui Zhang: Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing-Reviewing and Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21767011 and 22164010), Natural Science Foundation of Hunan province (No. 2020JJ4500).

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2022.124081.
